roles: Trudy Baggett - Diagnostician (ESC 11 conference attendee), Courtney Baker - Director of Special Education, Jerry - High School Diagnostician

extracts.quote: So, you know, often, like, the school psych and the Diag are working together on a case. Do we have access to the same child, or is she doing her own standalone, I'm doing my standalone, then we've got to converge those?
extracts.summary: Trudy asks about collaborative workflow between psychs and diags on shared cases

extracts.quote: Oh, I've looked at that, this is… yeah, don't mess with my stuff.
extracts.summary: Trudy expresses need for section locking to protect completed work in shared reports

extracts.quote: I love that, particularly, we just recently lost another… another… Honestly, Ms. Baker did a great job finding us another school psych, and then… what, two weeks later, one of the other ones quit
extracts.summary: Trudy describes ongoing staffing challenges with school psychologist turnover

extracts.quote: Of course, y'all know me, I'm a little bit of a control freak anyway. Like, that's the way I can see that I could help my psych, is like, girl, I'll upload all that stuff, I already, like, I send out their questionnaires for them.
extracts.summary: Trudy sees opportunity to support psychologists by handling document uploads as quarterback

extracts.quote: I mean, even if… because I know, like, Dr. Wagner has some AI, something that she's using that she has purchased, but, you know, she's having to put all that stuff up in there. If someone else did that for her, I'm thinking that's some time savings for her.
extracts.summary: Trudy notes colleague uses AI tool but still has to do manual uploads - sees efficiency opportunity

extracts.quote: Well, that, you know, I'm with… particularly down here with the young ones, Jerry, like, I mean, if the kid's been in school for 2 or 3, 5, 10, 20 years. Like, all that background stuff that you may tell the parent something they don't know in the background, but when we're starting with a 3-year-old, and really all the information we have is from ECI and the parents.
extracts.summary: Trudy describes limited background data available for early childhood evaluations vs older students

extracts.quote: Do you have any evaluators that give pushback of, like, no, I don't trust AI, or that's not the way we do it, or… you know what I mean?
extracts.summary: Trudy asks about evaluator resistance to AI adoption in other districts

extracts.quote: I can fill out my speech section, I don't need help with that. My time-sucking stuff is filling out all those other sections with information the teacher and the parents are giving me.
extracts.summary: Trudy describes SLP pain point - clinical sections easy but administrative sections take time

extracts.quote: So, what does it look like right now? Like, let's say it's an AU evaluation, and the speech person is a part of the team. What's that look like?
extracts.summary: Trudy asks how multi-disciplinary autism evaluations work with SLP involvement

extracts.quote: Besides me, that I can upload all that stuff, and I don't have to copy it all in there, that's for darn sure.
extracts.summary: Jerry excited about bulk document upload vs manual copying

extracts.quote: Anything to take the load off. I just want to make sure, because I know we used Marker last time for the evaluation piece. So, I wanted to just make sure, you know, everything is streamlined. To be more effective, yes.
extracts.summary: Courtney wants to ensure new platform streamlines work and integrates well with past Marker services

extracts.quote: You can send it over to me so I can just have a look at it, because we're getting ready to start budget season, so that I can, if it's something we're gonna move forward with, I can definitely have that in my planning purposes.
extracts.summary: Courtney requests contract for budget planning purposes

participants: Esther Osho, Courtney Patterson, Dr. Dahl Rollins
roles: Esther Osho - Director of School Psychologists, Courtney Patterson - School Psychology Coordinator (South/West), Dr. Dahl Rollins - School Psychology Coordinator (North/Central)

extracts.quote: Crazy! Just trying to wrap up, you know, before we go on break, so everything is expedited this, expedited that.
extracts.summary: Courtney describes end-of-semester pressure to rush evaluations before break

extracts.quote: No, no, no, no, we're not, we're not doing that this year.
extracts.summary: Courtney firmly states they won't take reports home over break - setting boundaries

extracts.quote: Same as Courtney. I'm the other coordinator, and then I support the North and Central Schools, so, all of the other half of the district.
extracts.summary: Dr. Dahl explains role split with Courtney - each covers half the district's schools

extracts.quote: I just have a quick question regarding the staff shortages. Was that nationwide, or was that specific to a region?
extracts.summary: Esther asks for clarification on whether staffing shortage data is national or regional

extracts.quote: With the template being copied, like you said, we can upload our own reports, does it just copy the structure of the template, or does it copy the, like, vernacular or language style we write in?
extracts.summary: Courtney asks if system learns both structure and writing style from uploaded reports

extracts.quote: And then I was just wondering if you guys provided, like, on-site training to staff members?
extracts.summary: Courtney asks about availability of in-person training for staff

extracts.quote: My question was also about the training, but that was answered. So, the other question I had was, I noticed when you showed the sample, you had a lot of PDFs. Are we required to upload PDFs, or how… What is the, you know, the file name? What can the files be?
extracts.summary: Dr. Dahl asks about supported file formats beyond PDFs

extracts.quote: No, those are the ones I was just checking to see, because a lot of people might just want to take a picture and upload it so JPEG would work, or a Word doc if they've already created something in a Word doc
extracts.summary: Dr. Dahl notes staff may prefer taking photos of documents or uploading Word files

extracts.quote: Also, I'm not sure if I missed it, but what's the turnaround time? So, if I'm a school psychologist, I, you know, upload all of the attachments and different things, what's the turnaround time for getting a draft report?
extracts.summary: Esther asks about report generation speed after uploading documents

extracts.quote: And then, in terms of the finished product, are we getting a possible conclusion and recommendations, or is it just interpretation?
extracts.summary: Esther asks if AI generates conclusions/recommendations or just summarizes data

extracts.quote: And then, what are we thinking in terms of cost? Do you guys provide any of that at this stage? Just so we can kind of get an idea of what we're paying contractors in comparison to maybe something like this?
extracts.summary: Esther asks about pricing to compare against contractor costs

extracts.quote: And then, last question for me. In terms of district-wide access, we do have other disciplines, of course, in SPED. Would this just be for school psychologists? Could this be expanded to, you know, DIAGs, SLPs, related services?
extracts.summary: Esther asks if platform extends beyond psychs to other SPED disciplines

extracts.quote: I like the pilot, being small, and really just doing a test run. We're a huge district, so I think sometimes doing a complete rollout with everybody can be a little stressful.
extracts.summary: Esther prefers small pilot approach given district size - full rollout too stressful

extracts.quote: My only concern is, with us being so big, the whole process to becoming a preferred provider can be a little long, so I'm not sure if we would actually even be ready, you know, next semester. But I'll send you that information, at least today, so that your team can maybe start working, and then we can just kind of go from there, okay?
extracts.summary: Esther warns preferred provider process is lengthy for large districts

extracts.quote: We'd probably have to work on that.
extracts.summary: Esther indicates budget would need to be secured/arranged for pilot

extracts.quote: Sorry, I just wanted to ask to expand on that. I know sometimes you can write, you know, just with ChatGPT. If you, like you said, if you know you're going into the writing of the report and say, write this to support an AU eligibility. Is that a possibility with Marker?
extracts.summary: Courtney asks if system can be directed to write toward specific eligibility determination like autism

roles: Brittany Mulkey - School Psychologist (5 years in district), Desiree - School Psychologist

extracts.quote: Pretty busy. We're… we're down a few staff members, so everybody's caseload's kind of doubled, maybe tripled a little bit, so we're, you know, navigating that and growing pains going along with that.
extracts.summary: Brittany describes severe staffing shortage causing doubled/tripled caseloads

extracts.quote: It is unusual. Well, our… our numbers grew from when I first got here, as far as, like, students that we're supporting, like, everyone's special education numbers are growing from our… our child find, our early childhood, all the way throughout
extracts.summary: Brittany notes special ed numbers growing across all levels from early childhood up

extracts.quote: Texas is a little different. We used to not service students that only required dyslexia support through special education, but a couple of years ago, the laws in Texas changed, so those students used to just be serviced under 504, and now they're serviced through special education, so numbers started to grow pretty quickly.
extracts.summary: Brittany explains Texas law change moved dyslexia students from 504 to SPED, driving caseload growth

extracts.quote: A lot of our stuff is definitely going to be handwritten from the referral packet, so that is nice that it recognizes handwriting. We haven't, I don't think, advanced in all of our campuses yet to… Being fully gripped.
extracts.summary: Brittany notes most referral docs are handwritten - campuses not fully digital yet

extracts.quote: Handwritten notes from teachers and parents filling out forms that are given to them.
extracts.summary: Brittany clarifies handwriting comes from teacher and parent forms, not just evaluator notes

extracts.quote: We have multi-disciplinary reports, so we would be inputting things from speech pathologists, occupational therapists, so it… For different disciplines, it would still…
extracts.summary: Brittany asks about handling multi-disciplinary reports with SLP/OT contributions

extracts.quote: I think for us trying to figure out how to kind of sell this to the powers that be for our budget, the assessment staff for special education for a district the size of La Porte. So La Porte's, like, what, 8,000 students. So, our assessment staff number is, for Sykes and Diags, 13, 14.
extracts.summary: Brittany concerned about justifying cost for small assessment staff of 13-14 people

extracts.quote: We have OTs, PTs, speech. If we include all of them, maybe we're at 30. So I know it's, this is a big money investment for 30 assessment professionals, but I think one of the ways we… if we could make it appealing to our special education teachers as case managers, I think right now might be the only way to kind of… sell this to being something that's efficient
extracts.summary: Brittany suggests extending to case managers might help justify cost for 30 total staff

extracts.quote: because we have a report writing software now, and it is for the year. Of course, it's not anywhere near this, but I think it didn't even cost $2,000, and that was a bag to get.
extracts.summary: Brittany mentions existing report software costs under $2K - even that was hard to get approved

extracts.quote: we do budget, as a district quite a bit for contract employees, and so we are spending a lot of money on contractors. Yes. And so that… that could be an area, too, that we…
extracts.summary: Brittany notes significant contractor spending that could be redirected

extracts.quote: We don't have to… we have… Five. We have five, and then we have… diagnosticians? 7… 5? I don't know. We have… we have… we have five school psychologists, and then we have 6 or 7 diagnosticians. It might not even be that, right?
extracts.summary: Brittany confirms 5 psychologists and 6-7 diagnosticians - uncertainty shows staffing flux

extracts.quote: And we definitely spend way more than that in contracting. Way, way more.
extracts.summary: Brittany confirms contractor spending far exceeds proposed $33K platform cost

extracts.quote: So, one with, like, say for, like, the WISC, for example, it'll generate the narrative on the test. If you uploaded the actual, like, item analysis of the test. Will it kind of break that down, too, and tell you which items they performed well on, what type of items they did not?
extracts.summary: Brittany asks if system can analyze item-level WISC performance beyond standard scores

extracts.quote: Okay, so we need good pre-referral documents to make sure we're scanning for everybody to get the most benefit out of this.
extracts.summary: Brittany realizes quality pre-referral docs improve AI output quality

extracts.quote: And it translates reports, because I know, like, our… we have Success Ed, and it will only change the static information to Spanish. It won't change the actual text that's inputted by evaluators.
extracts.summary: Brittany notes current system Success Ed only translates static fields, not evaluator text

extracts.quote: And so then, if you edit it and say, like, this isn't really how I would write this, I'm gonna say it this way, does it kind of learn for the next time?
extracts.summary: Brittany asks if system learns from user edits to improve future reports

extracts.quote: And it does that per user, or, like, as a district example?
extracts.summary: Brittany asks if learning is per-user or district-wide

extracts.quote: Saving the 10K helps, because I don't think that would have gone through this year to try, but if we try it, and we can prove how much more, because we have a list of overdue things that we need to get done, and it can help in that process, too, and the cost… the savings cost for contract staff would be another one to actually, I think, sell the affordability or sell, like, the time that we're getting back, and all of that, I think would be super helpful.
extracts.summary: Brittany says waived trial fee helps - will use overdue evals and contractor savings to justify

extracts.quote: I think what would do it is we would collectively have to agree to one of those positions not being filled. We always try to fill them. They haven't been getting filled, but if this will save us so much time and money, and we're okay with one of those not being filled to pay for this, we could sell it. We could sell it.
extracts.summary: Brittany suggests redirecting unfilled position budget to fund platform adoption

participants: Tracy Schluter, Deanna Hicks, Kelly Mock, Karin (absent)
roles: Tracy Schluter - Director of Psychological Services, Deanna Hicks - Coordinator for Diagnosticians (125 FTEs), Kelly Mock - Coordinator for Speech Pathologists (189 FTEs)

extracts.quote: Absolutely, yeah, facing all of those things, as you know, our numbers for assessment are higher than they've ever been, and, you know, turnover is prevalent, you know, having to have contract workers come in that, you know, are not always 100%
extracts.summary: Team confirms assessment numbers at all-time high with turnover and unreliable contractors

extracts.quote: in our district is a struggle, so there's all of those, you know, pieces. And you want to do a great report and spend a lot of great time on it, but you have, you know, 17 others sitting next to you, too. And every student is you know, equally important
extracts.summary: Team describes struggle of wanting quality reports while having 17+ waiting in queue

extracts.quote: just like your story, you know, is invaluable, because you want to do the best you can for each of those students. But there's only so many hours in a day, days in a week, etc.
extracts.summary: Team emphasizes desire to help each student fully but time constraints limit them

extracts.quote: And I have the dyslexia testing that we're doing.
extracts.summary: Kelly notes additional dyslexia testing adding to workload

extracts.quote: We have 124 diagnosticians. Yeah. 189 SLPs? And I have about 70-ish Psychologist and 10 doctoral interns.
extracts.summary: Team provides staffing numbers: 124 diags, 189 SLPs, 70 psychs, 10 interns

extracts.quote: About right? Yeah.
extracts.summary: Team confirms 12,000 evals per year and 10-hour report writing time estimates are accurate

extracts.quote: I went to grad school with Blair. Tell her you met Tracy today. Yes, we go way back. So, when that caught my attention when you said Prosper. So, yes, I'd love to talk to her about it, and definitely will.
extracts.summary: Tracy knows Blair from grad school - will reach out for reference on Prosper experience

extracts.quote: We're kind of… we're kind of bound by our software management system, so do other districts copy and paste sections into their software management? Because that's kind of where we are at at the moment.
extracts.summary: Tracy asks about integration with existing software management system - currently doing copy-paste

extracts.quote: Can you add in, like, you get a piece of information later, and you What does that look like?
extracts.summary: Tracy asks how to add late-arriving information to existing reports

extracts.quote: Did you say you could do it with multiple evaluators in one report? Is that what y'all just said? highlight and lock certain sections, so I'm not tripping on your, you know, I'm not giving to you, and you're not, you know, I'm not messing up some of you did.
extracts.summary: Tracy confirms understanding of multi-evaluator collaboration with section locking

extracts.quote: Stefan, did you… did you say what the cost per report was? Did I miss that?
extracts.summary: Tracy asks about per-report pricing model

extracts.quote: 100 different languages and dialects, so we got…
extracts.summary: Kelly notes Cypress has students speaking 100+ different languages - translation need is huge

extracts.quote: And then, what about recommendations? Do you leave that to the clinician as well?
extracts.summary: Kelly asks if recommendation section is AI-generated or left to clinician

extracts.quote: Sounds wonderful, yeah.
extracts.summary: Kelly expresses positive reaction to AI-assisted recommendations with clinical oversight

extracts.quote: Where are you guys at with the… I know you'd said that, primarily Diag report, psych report, so where are you at with the speech path report?
extracts.summary: Kelly asks about SLP functionality status - wants to know timeline

extracts.quote: So, how long is the training… how long does that take?
extracts.summary: Team asks about training duration

extracts.quote: I don't know, Marshall's not in the room. Yeah, we need to… we need to look at… talk about our budget a little more. That… and… and compare that to what we've paid for, any… any… off-contract report writing, I guess.
extracts.summary: Tracy notes budget decision requires Marshall (Asst Super) and comparison to contract writing costs

extracts.quote: And see if… if that is comparable, and if so… I mean, it certainly is a time saver, and our staff would love it, and I'm competent of that. It's just whether or not, you know, the district's gonna find the money going forward, and the pilot sounds great. So, I think… I think we could definitely come up with the people for the pilot.
extracts.summary: Tracy confident staff would love it - just needs budget approval - can identify pilot participants

extracts.quote: Let Karen in on what she missed, as well. And Marshawn. Do y'all know… have you met Marshawn, Stefan? Marshall Peters is our Assistant Superintendent of our Educational Support Services, so she's… The, assistant superintendent that Karen and I, as directors, report to.
extracts.summary: Tracy identifies Marshall Peters as Asst Super - key decision maker they report to

roles: Angel Royal - School Psychologist (tenured, multiple schools), Adjunct Professor at Drexel, Private Practice Owner, AI Advocate for 160-person psych team

extracts.quote: It's been okay. It's been busy, but I mean, it's always busy. That's just the way it goes.
extracts.summary: Angel describes perpetually busy workload as the norm

extracts.quote: That's so true. Yeah. Yeah. That's probably the way my teams would describe me.
extracts.summary: Angel agrees she's the go-to problem solver who handles anything

extracts.quote: I mean, I work as a school psychologist in Philly. I'm assigned to a couple schools. I mentor a couple of the psychologists. That's kind of been one of the things that I've been doing pretty consistently now is mentoring.
extracts.summary: Angel describes multi-faceted role: direct psych work, multiple schools, mentoring colleagues

extracts.quote: But like Charlotte, we're always trying to find a way out. So we both teach at Drexle. so I have had the opportunity to teach one of the school site courses. So Drexel I think they're in their third cohort now of school psych students.
extracts.summary: Angel teaches at Drexel - she and Charlotte looking for paths beyond district work

extracts.quote: So I do that and then also I have a practice with a business partner which that keeps me busy with just trying to do everything so yes and we really want to increase our capacity
extracts.summary: Angel runs private practice with partner and wants to expand capacity

extracts.quote: However, I am very very close friends with my supervisor. So, I'm always like, 'You need to do this, and' So, yeah.
extracts.summary: Angel has strong relationship with supervisor - can influence decisions informally

extracts.quote: Yeah. I think it's phenomenal. I absolutely love it. And I love that you have the citations there. I think that is extremely helpful.
extracts.summary: Angel loves the platform especially the citation feature for verification

extracts.quote: and I could see how for districts, it should be very enticing for districts because to cut down on the contractors if you're employed, psychologists are able to do more work. I mean the need for contractors wouldn't be there which I think would help so many problems
extracts.summary: Angel sees value proposition of reducing contractor dependency through increased psych productivity

extracts.quote: because there was a study that was done in filling a few years ago. Not a few. It was a little while ago, but one thing that they said that that came from the findings is that anything related to special education, we really actually needed to keep it house that we shouldn't have contractors because we have contractors that kind of came in and they caused more damage than good
extracts.summary: Angel cites Philly study finding contractors caused more problems than they solved in SPED

extracts.quote: No, I mean they eventually went ahead and hired contractors even though the findings the recommendation was not to… but they still did it. But they currently have something with contractors and I think it's not going as well as they hoped.
extracts.summary: Angel says district hired contractors despite study warning - current arrangement struggling

extracts.quote: So they have an evaluation center that just has contracted psychologists and they're actually having trouble staffing that where the agency reaches out to us. We were like, 'Well, we can't do it.'
extracts.summary: Angel describes contracted evaluation center that can't find staff - agencies asking district psychs to help

extracts.quote: So, I think I would love to, run it by her and then, see if it's something. I know a lot of times we get caught up with of course funding just sometimes a lot of different things within Philly, like it and the different layers of being in a large school district.
extracts.summary: Angel will pitch to supervisor but notes Philly's complex bureaucracy and funding challenges

extracts.quote: it's about 160.
extracts.summary: Angel's supervisor oversees approximately 160 school psychologists

extracts.quote: No. It doesn't sound too crazy at all. No. Let's do it after the holiday. Yeah, that would be good.
extracts.summary: Angel agrees to follow-up meeting after holiday - open to moving forward

extracts.quote: some of the things around AI. I've been the one, to talk to her about it. I've gone to workshops and different things like that on AI and so she knows this is something I'm interested in.
extracts.summary: Angel is the AI advocate on the team - supervisor knows of her AI interest from past workshops

participants: Quincy Meeker, Jill Miller, Rebecca Cybulski
roles: Quincy Meeker - Diagnostician, Jill Miller - Diagnostician (Middle School, Communications focus), Rebecca Cybulski - Diagnostician (High School)

extracts.quote: I have been using it, but typically I'm just using it right now for the charts. it's just easier to bring those reports in and have the system create the chart for me than to just type the numbers into a Google doc myself.
extracts.summary: Quincy primarily uses platform for chart generation - finds it easier than manual data entry

extracts.quote: if I import both at the same time, it combines teacher and parent into one paragraph. And I prefer to have those things separated in the report to just show very clearly what the parent versus teacher is saying.
extracts.summary: Quincy wants parent and teacher input separated rather than combined in narrative

extracts.quote: And so if I'm wanting the system to kind of do that for me, I'm having to import one, copy it out, then import the other, copy it out, delete it.
extracts.summary: Quincy describes cumbersome workaround to get separated parent/teacher sections

extracts.quote: I've been using it primarily for that for kind of giving me some starting points for narratives about parent and teacher information. I have not been using it for templates because y'all don't have the tests that I give.
extracts.summary: Rebecca uses platform only for narrative starters - missing high school assessments limits utility

extracts.quote: So, I work in high school and I primarily give the waste five or the WJ5 and you've got either of those. So, it has not been helpful at all for me in terms of the actual testing.
extracts.summary: Rebecca cannot use platform for assessments - WAIS-5 and WJ-5 not supported

extracts.quote: No, you have The waist 5 has been out for almost a year. So, we've been using it since last September.
extracts.summary: Rebecca corrects that WAIS-5 has been available for a year - platform behind on updates

extracts.quote: It doesn't. So, I tried importing waist five scores as waist 4 and it created nothing for me. I did the same with the WJ4 because even the subtest names are the same for some of the achievement. And I tried uploading a WJ5 score report for a WJ in your WJ4 and it didn't recognize any of the scores and didn't create a template for me.
extracts.summary: Rebecca tried workarounds uploading WAIS-5 as WAIS-4 - system created nothing

extracts.quote: high school is not using that at all because you don't like not especially not for Cobb because you don't have the test we give.
extracts.summary: High school staff not using platform due to missing assessment support

extracts.quote: it tends to put all of the information in every single paragraph. So, they pass their vision and hearing screenings or they take medication for ADHD and that's in the referral section and the speech and language section and the physical section and the behavior section and the cog section and the achievement section and I don't need to repeat it that many times.
extracts.summary: Rebecca frustrated by repetitive information appearing in every section of report

extracts.quote: So just taking the report from marker and importing it, it will not work at all because it's just a bunch of repetitive information and it's not always correct.
extracts.summary: Rebecca says reports cannot be used as-is - too repetitive and contains errors

extracts.quote: The parent and teacher forms from Embrace that we use from our gathering those inputs uses a liyker scale of one to five and then an n for not applicable for all of these different criteria all of these different items it's all one through five and for some reason consistently and I've used it probably 15 times it does not recognize the scale of one to five.
extracts.summary: Rebecca reports Embrace forms with 1-5 Likert scale consistently not recognized after 15 attempts

extracts.quote: So, it will write sentences they got a two out of five. it's clearly delineated on the form that two is below average they'll interpret the N for not applicable as a no. So, it'll say the student cannot perform math computations when really it was just the English teacher completing that form and declined to comment on their math skills because they don't teach them math.
extracts.summary: Rebecca describes AI misinterpreting N/A as 'no' and not understanding Likert scale meanings

extracts.quote: chat GPT can do it for me much easier. the caveat with chat GPT is that I have to take out all the personally identifiable information.
extracts.summary: Rebecca finds ChatGPT easier to use but recognizes PII removal requirement

extracts.quote: I had understood that marker was using AI in that way and I expected it to learn and so I guess I was wrong about that.
extracts.summary: Rebecca expected system to learn from repeated use - disappointed it doesn't

extracts.quote: One of the areas I've been using it in is comms. and with my communication self-contained students, I've been using the ABAS, which has been incredibly helpful, not only for the graphs. it's an adaptive thing and so parents like circle, the kids are able to do.
extracts.summary: Jill finds ABAS support incredibly helpful for communication self-contained students

extracts.quote: So, my first and second year, I think I literally went through and was like, the kid can do this. they can't do this. And so it has been incredibly helpful to be, it explains each one. It's got the score. it does the parents versus the teacher.
extracts.summary: Jill contrasts manual process from early career with current AI efficiency on ABAS

extracts.quote: I really wish there was a way where you could put in their diagnosis because the autism down below where it's like the summary and conclusion like the autism one is usually what my kids are and it definitely fits. It'll sometimes have an OI in there and I'm like that's not really what I'm looking at.
extracts.summary: Jill wants ability to input diagnosis so summary aligns with actual eligibility category

extracts.quote: I don't think it has slowed me down at all. I don't think it takes more time to do that than it would to plug it into the draft. And the charts are made, so that's nice.
extracts.summary: Jill confirms platform doesn't slow her down and chart generation is valuable

extracts.quote: Most of us high school people aren't really using it for the charts and stuff because it doesn't have those tests. and then like I said, I don't know if it's user error, but I can't get the achievement to work either if I'm just doing abbreviated assessments, which I am doing since most of high school is rehabs and I'm not doing the full of anything at this point.
extracts.summary: Rebecca notes high school staff can't use charts due to missing assessments - abbreviated tests don't work

extracts.quote: it does not help me with writing up previous assessments or that kind of thing which when we were originally trained I asked that question can I use it for a review of existing evaluation data and I was told yes this would be great and it does not create any kind of blurbs about previous evaluation
extracts.summary: Rebecca was told platform would help with previous eval reviews but it doesn't work

extracts.quote: I mean I had the system tell me that this student had autism and ID and dyslexia and speech impairment and all the things that they didn't have. They were autism and that was like it and I was like where is it getting this information? Maybe because the word dyslexia is on the form but it's check no
extracts.summary: Rebecca describes system incorrectly stating student had multiple disabilities when only autism applied

extracts.quote: I think if it were able to read the parent and teacher input correctly, more people would use it. I think there's a fear of not correcting something or not catching something and it's wrong and you send it to parents who go over through it with a fine tooth comment are like why did you say my student is this when they're
extracts.summary: Quincy says fear of uncaught errors going to parents prevents broader adoption

extracts.quote: And I will say too, just to kind of add on to that, I should have mentioned it before, is that again in high school and probably in middle school as well, we have multiple teacher reports. And I would like it to be able to combine that information and saying instead of saying this sentence right now says that Bin is rated as poor in completing tasks on time, coming to class prepared and staying on task. However, some reports indicate she completes task on time and comes to class prepared at an average level and she is on task most of the time.
extracts.summary: Rebecca wants system to synthesize conflicting teacher reports intelligently rather than contradicting itself

extracts.quote: And so sometimes teachers have a different view of a student in different subject areas or at different times of day etc. And I would like it to acknowledge that instead of saying she is on task and she is not on task which is not helpful.
extracts.summary: Rebecca wants system to acknowledge teacher perspective differences rather than contradicting itself

extracts.quote: I have a spreadsheet that creates one for me. I've never actually inserted it into a report, but I sometimes use it when I'm meeting with parents for an initial or that kind of thing to just kind of give that visual of how things stack up and compare and contrast and all of that.
extracts.summary: Rebecca already has spreadsheet for bell curves - uses for parent meetings not reports

extracts.quote: I think it would be most helpful like Rebecca was saying for parents to visualize instead of just throwing numbers at them. I have not particularly used those in my reports. but I'd have to play with it. and see if that's something I want to use or at least just have available for review with parents.
extracts.summary: Quincy sees bell curve value for parent communication rather than report embedding

extracts.quote: that reabal piece would be huge for us. if we could get it to read a previous report and at least give us summaries in each of those headings of what the previous report said in cognitive and what the previous report said was done in achievement or whatever. yeah, if we could get it to recognize and summarize that information for us, that would be huge.
extracts.summary: Rebecca says re-eval summary feature pulling from previous reports would be huge for high school

extracts.quote: in the middle school, we do a lot of dyslexia testing and that's a big one that they use. because the Ctops on there, I think I saw the rats on there.
extracts.summary: Jill notes GORT important for middle school dyslexia testing - CTOPs and WRAT available

extracts.quote: The worm's not though. Will the rat work for the wormmet? Wr3 is not on here at all.
extracts.summary: Rebecca asks about WRMT-3 support - not currently available

extracts.quote: the RAS 2 has been out for 10 years. Is that really the Rio 2 or is it just the Rio? Because yeah, it has been out since a long time ago. that's another one that I use at high school because it's a brief but it's an adultnormed assessment. So that is another one that I use especially with my lower functioning populations and my adults because I have our 18 plus program on my campus.
extracts.summary: Rebecca uses RIAS-2 for adult students in 18+ program - needs this assessment

roles: Ashley Noble - Coordinator of School Psychologists (TASP Board Member), Shauntee Carpenter - Coordinator of Diagnosticians

extracts.quote: There's 600 people. it's a lot and I don't know if you've ever put an event on with a hotel. Not always the most fun process. and also who I texted people on the board yesterday and I was like, is there a bet circling around because I also was assigned a virtual PD session for 3 hours on how to have, a successful internship and early years experience.
extracts.summary: Ashley describes overwhelming TASP conference responsibilities - 600 attendees plus presenting

extracts.quote: it's my first year putting this conference on the board and also am somehow supposed to present and get that done. So I was up here all day yesterday for eight hours at the office. Still didn't get it recorded.
extracts.summary: Ashley worked 8 hours on weekend and still couldn't complete conference prep

extracts.quote: I don't think at this point sooner than that. I don't think how it's going right now. Budgets are weird and I know they're having personnel conversations earlier than they usually do. but we'll let y'all know if conversations start to get bumped up earlier.
extracts.summary: Ashley notes budget timing uncertain - personnel conversations happening earlier than usual

extracts.quote: the data, which is probably pretty easy for y'all to pull at any time, is just the stuff that you're sharing right now of the impact that's what they're going to want to see.
extracts.summary: Ashley confirms leadership wants to see impact data for renewal decisions

extracts.quote: I'm kind of surprised that it's Becky. I'm a little surprised by Beverly. Beverly is a more traditional, been in the field a while, school psych. So, I'm excited to see that she's incorporating that she is known for writing really solid reports that are pretty in-depth and so I'm glad to see her using it.
extracts.summary: Ashley surprised and pleased that veteran school psych Beverly is actively using platform

extracts.quote: So each itinerant team has leads. Michelle is one of the two school psych leads and then you may have met Jessica, she's the other one. and then DAGs have two leads, none of which are on that same on the school psych. I'm not inclined to put leads on this just because just for your knowledge base they have full case loads and they already carry so much extra stuff I don't want to add.
extracts.summary: Ashley explains lead structure but won't overburden leads with additional platform responsibilities

extracts.quote: in our world sometimes no feedback is good feedback.
extracts.summary: Ashley notes lack of complaints from team is generally positive signal

extracts.quote: what I'm hearing is that parent and teacher information forms that they're uploading, it's giving completely inaccurate information and it may not be the entire piece, but it's definitely portions and they're trying to figure out where that information came from.
extracts.summary: Shauntee reports team finding completely inaccurate info from parent/teacher form uploads

extracts.quote: I had one diag tell me recently she was just like, I will never use it again because I just didn't catch it. And so, she was just mortified unfortunately. but she can overreact a little bit. So, just take that with a grain of salt. But definitely something we want to work on.
extracts.summary: Shauntee reports diagnostician saying she'll never use platform again after missing an error

extracts.quote: And Audrey, when she says, 'A bit of an over reaction at times.' That was an understatement and that was the nicest way that she just put that and I adore her so I can say that lovingly
extracts.summary: Ashley confirms the diagnostician tends to overreact but the concern is still valid

roles: Jennifer Blake - Director of Special Education Assessment, Stacey Lindsay - School Psychology Coordinator

extracts.quote: users felt it was too much of a copycat, losing some AI and marker technology efficiencies
extracts.summary: Oakland team feels current template too closely mirrors district guide - loses AI benefits

extracts.quote: there is skill variability in understanding what AI should do
extracts.summary: Jennifer notes staff have varying understanding of AI capabilities and expectations

extracts.quote: Fabi Ramirez, Jocelyn Roundtree, and Kate, in addition to Latoya, due to their respected positions, quality of reports, and collegial support roles
extracts.summary: Jennifer recommends specific staff for template rework based on respect, quality, and influence

extracts.quote: Chelsea, one of the Early Childhood Education (ECE) team members, who works off a different template
extracts.summary: Jennifer recommends including ECE representative who uses different template

extracts.quote: Stacey Lindsay suggesting Monday, Tuesday, or Wednesday mornings, while Jennifer Blake indicated they would likely only join a meeting toward the end of the school year for longitudinal data
extracts.summary: Jennifer wants longitudinal data review at year end rather than frequent check-ins

roles: Erin Priour - Coordinator of Child Find Services (newly promoted)

extracts.quote: a lot of this is coming from guidance that's being pushed out in some of the trainings that our staff members are attending. And that guidance honestly is muddy because that is the land of AI. I think you're building the plane while you're flying it kind of thing.
extracts.summary: Erin describes AI guidance as muddy - everyone building the plane while flying it

extracts.quote: what APA is saying is if you are putting data into AI if AI is analyzing any type of data then you as a practitioner should be disclosing your use of AI.
extracts.summary: Erin cites APA guidance requiring disclosure when AI analyzes data

extracts.quote: some of these companies are also saying and you should be asking for parental consent there should be a consent component where you not only disclose your use of it but you give the parents the opportunity to decide if they want AI involved in their child's report or not.
extracts.summary: Erin notes some guidance suggests parental consent for AI use in reports

extracts.quote: When Kurt and I met about it I know he and I are kind of on the same page with I just don't think that consent is required. we're not using AI to make decisions for us. AI is a tool that is part of helping us do our jobs more efficiently, but it's not a clinician. It's not a decision maker.
extracts.summary: Erin and Kurt agree consent not required - AI is tool not decision-maker

extracts.quote: with Expedited, we are not including a disclosure statement even though they are also utilizing AI. But their report writers are all licensed psychologists. So they have licensed professionals who are writing the report with the help of AI then giving it to us who are editing the report as clinicians with knowledge of the student
extracts.summary: Erin explains no disclosure for Expedited because licensed psychs review before delivery

extracts.quote: with marker because you guys don't know the kids you are using AI as this tool to synthesize information we were like okay yeah I guess in that case we do need to put some kind of disclosure
extracts.summary: Erin differentiates Marker needing disclosure because no prior human review of student

extracts.quote: This psychological evaluation was prepared with the assistance of artificial intelligence technology to support report organization, initial drafting, and formatting. All clinical interpretations, findings, and recommendations have been thoroughly reviewed, verified, and approved by a licensed school psychologist to ensure accuracy and professional standards. The use of AI technology enhances efficiency while maintaining the highest level of clinical expertise and ethical practice in service to your child's educational needs.
extracts.summary: Erin shares proposed AI disclosure statement emphasizing clinical review and professional standards

extracts.quote: I think we would embed it in the report I think probably at the end it's just kind of a generic disclosure statement whenever we're utilizing marker.
extracts.summary: Erin plans to embed disclosure at end of reports using Marker

extracts.quote: I feel like right now we're not ready to have those, arguments with parents about whether or not we're going to allow the use of AI in their child's report.
extracts.summary: Erin wants to avoid parent consent debates - not ready for those arguments

extracts.quote: right now that's the direction we're going, but we're not looking for consent.
extracts.summary: Erin confirms direction is disclosure not consent

extracts.quote: I looked at NAS the NASA has a task force right now for AI but they have not yet released any specific guidance. The only specific guidance I could find was from the APA.
extracts.summary: Erin researched NASP - no guidance yet, only APA has specifics

extracts.quote: PAR just did a series of webinars on ethical use of AI and evaluations. because they're launching their own AI report writing platform. and so I can't remember if it's three or four, but they did a three or four part series
extracts.summary: Erin attended PAR webinars on AI ethics - they're launching competing platform

extracts.quote: they had different presenters and even within their different presenters there were conflicting viewpoints. So I'm like, I don't know what the right answer is, but the noise is you need to be addressing it somehow.
extracts.summary: Erin notes even PAR presenters had conflicting views - consensus is to address it somehow

roles: Stephen - Assistant Director of Special Education (former school psychologist)

extracts.quote: retention is their biggest struggle. 2 bigger neighboring districts with more money. they have vacancies which means that it puts more workload on their people who remain
extracts.summary: Stephen identifies retention as biggest challenge - competing with wealthier neighbors

extracts.quote: needs to be a 'game changer' to justify the price point. what does 'game changer' mean? that they are not struggling to keep up with their caseload, they are reporting that this is taking so much less time, they aren't doing work at home, they have more time to actually leverage their expertise
extracts.summary: Stephen defines game changer as: manageable caseload, less time, no work at home, use expertise

extracts.quote: Used school psych ai last year but did not have a good experience
extracts.summary: Stephen notes bad experience with competitor School Psych AI last year

roles: Mallory - Coordinator of Evaluations (former lead diagnostician for 3 years, diagnostician for 16 years)

extracts.quote: the talent they are getting for diags and psychs is poor and requiring her to do a lot of review and coaching. has had to do more growth plans in the last 3 years than ever. simple mistakes that are coming through that make reports not grammatically correct or professional
extracts.summary: Mallory notes declining talent quality requiring more coaching and growth plans than ever

extracts.quote: allen does not allow work from home and she said most other districts do which is a limiting factor for her hiring
extracts.summary: Mallory identifies no-WFH policy as hiring disadvantage vs other districts

extracts.quote: requested 3 diags and is planning on using budget to get 2 and then the rest reserved for marker. trading 1 FTE 80k+
extracts.summary: Mallory planning to trade 1 FTE position for Marker subscription

extracts.quote: mentioned concerns about older diags being comfortable with leveraging an AI tool and what impact that could have on her hiring
extracts.summary: Mallory concerned about older staff comfort with AI affecting hiring decisions

roles: Teri - Complex Area Lead (handles contract/finance), Amanda - School Psychologist, Iris - School Psychologist

extracts.quote: Teri core reason to use the platform is to give her team a life outside of work, hobbies, not work weekends
extracts.summary: Teri's primary goal is giving team work-life balance - hobbies, no weekend work

extracts.quote: assessment rat race
extracts.summary: Teri describes current state as 'assessment rat race'

extracts.quote: 10 hours baseline report writing, this varies based on what assessments they do
extracts.summary: Team confirms 10 hour baseline for report writing with variation by assessment type

extracts.quote: Iris wants to spend more time supporting students and teachers and her teams. wants to do the things that have more of a day to day difference
extracts.summary: Iris wants to shift time from reports to direct student/teacher support

extracts.quote: Other data they would be interested in is is the platform making their reports more readable and accessible? is it making their reports more defensible?
extracts.summary: Team wants to measure readability, accessibility, and defensibility improvements

extracts.quote: the team is all on 12 month contracts so they don't get anything but govt holidays
extracts.summary: Hawaii team on 12-month contracts with only government holidays - no summer break

roles: Mindy - School Psychology Coordinator, Andrew - Special Education Administrator

extracts.quote: overall very positive from the team so far, less stressed, reduced mental load, makes their reports more robust and comprehensive
extracts.summary: Team reporting positive outcomes: less stress, reduced mental load, better reports

extracts.quote: learning curve for some people. student data concerns
extracts.summary: Mindy notes learning curve challenges and data security concerns from some staff

extracts.quote: school team referral to assessment team - this is one document that people are sharing the system is having some issues with. same with the MTSS form
extracts.summary: Team reports issues with referral and MTSS form document processing

extracts.quote: Gender incorrect - Katie Gerard KABC. rating scales write over. handwriting not uploading
extracts.summary: Mindy shares specific bugs: wrong gender, rating scales overwriting, handwriting upload failures

extracts.quote: Requested adding a post High School drop down for grade to help with transition programs since they work with student until age 21
extracts.summary: Team requests post-high school grade option for transition program students up to age 21

extracts.quote: 1 user in the group a key influencer who thinks they should need to notify parents that they are using AI
extracts.summary: Key influencer on team believes parents should be notified about AI use

extracts.quote: Headline overall right now is that its taking them more time to edit and review then it is to use
extracts.summary: Nancy reports editing and review taking more time than just using the platform

extracts.quote: Feels like we are making improvements but that its just baby steps to getting there
extracts.summary: Nancy acknowledges improvements but describes progress as baby steps

extracts.quote: Savannah said she is drowning and its easier to do what she knows when she needs to get through a bunch of testing
extracts.summary: Staff member Savannah too overwhelmed to learn new system - reverting to known methods

extracts.quote: one date keeps populating backwards. one subtest description does not match
extracts.summary: Nancy notes specific bugs: date formatting and subtest description mismatch

extracts.quote: TX passed a law where students get $6k funding for public school, $10k funding for private/home school and extra $$ if they are in special education, seeing 100s of requests come in for IEPs
extracts.summary: Curt describes Texas voucher law driving hundreds of new IEP requests

extracts.quote: They are not compliant as a district, they reached 85% last year with initials but missed 100%
extracts.summary: Curt admits district not compliant - only hit 85% on initial eval timelines

extracts.quote: white is the minority in their district and over represented in sped. 85% hispanic in their district
extracts.summary: Curt notes disproportionality concern - white students over-represented in SPED despite being minority

extracts.quote: would also like to see us flag if there are conflicting pieces in a report for example from an SLP portion and a psych portion so that way someone can go in an explain why so that it looks like the team really did coordinate together to write the report for the student and it stays cohesive
extracts.summary: Curt wants system to flag conflicting info between SLP and psych sections for cohesion

extracts.quote: he never makes anything on his team mandatory in terms of using support services but they cannot refuse help and support if they are not meeting deadlines and not in compliance
extracts.summary: Curt doesn't mandate tools but non-compliant staff can't refuse support

extracts.quote: he loved the bell curve - shared he spends a lot of time training on this - it helps teachers too not just parents, tables are not enough
extracts.summary: Curt loves bell curve feature - spends training time on this, helps teachers and parents

group: Special Education / School Psychologists Focus Group
roles: Monique - School Psychologist (part-time), Brenda - School Psychologist, Tess - School Psychologist, Beverly - Senior School Psychologist

extracts.quote: likes school psych AI better, Sophia - more flexible, marker is too rigid, likes giving it commands on what to do. FBA and BIPs - uses sophia for these too
extracts.summary: Monique prefers School Psych AI/Sophia for flexibility - uses it for FBAs and BIPs

extracts.quote: referral and background info biggest use case - quick writeup. uses the rating scales to some extent, likes the tables but wants the range in the tables
extracts.summary: Monique uses Marker for referral/background writeups and rating scale tables - wants ranges added

extracts.quote: downloads and then copies things over because she mainly uses it for the tables. screenshots the tables then from her document and adds that then into embrace
extracts.summary: Monique screenshots tables from download to paste into Embrace system

extracts.quote: faster now than it was last year. wants to be able to drag and input documents only into those specific sections where now she feels like the documents populate all over the place vs. just in the spots she wants to see them in
extracts.summary: Brenda notes improvement over last year but wants document placement control by section

extracts.quote: loves the summary at the end. writes the whole report in a gdoc and then copies the whole thing into marker just to use the summary
extracts.summary: Brenda loves summary feature so much she writes reports in Google Docs then pastes just for summary

extracts.quote: diags she has heard also don't know where info is going into what places
extracts.summary: Brenda reports diagnosticians confused about where uploaded info appears in reports

extracts.quote: thinks the narratives that generate right now are short and minimal and she has to add a lot to them
extracts.summary: Brenda finds generated narratives too short and minimal - requires significant additions

extracts.quote: asked if the system is set to always think that a student needs services - felt like it often defaults to this
extracts.summary: Brenda questions if system biased toward recommending services

extracts.quote: wants to see the descriptors for rating scales in the tables too. uses marker the most for table generation
extracts.summary: Tess wants rating scale descriptors in tables - table generation is primary use case

extracts.quote: summary right now shows that OHI is for every student - thinks she may not be updating something correctly
extracts.summary: Tess sees OHI appearing for every student in summary - unsure if user error

extracts.quote: wouldn't let her input two teacher rating scale files for the SSIS
extracts.summary: Tess couldn't upload multiple teacher rating scales for SSIS

extracts.quote: mentioned a diag who shared there was inaccurate information that populates regarding a students medical issues and medications
extracts.summary: Tess reports diagnostician found inaccurate medical/medication info in generated reports

extracts.quote: just getting used to the platform. thought the summary and conclusions section was based on the referral documents (lack of education here)
extracts.summary: Beverly new to platform - misunderstood summary section as based on referral docs

extracts.quote: has felt like she is rewriting most things, information has not been pulling from assessment information
extracts.summary: Beverly feels she's rewriting most content - assessment info not pulling correctly

extracts.quote: wants separate parent and teacher information in the emotional/behavioral section - does parent and then deletes it and then does teacher to get clean narratives the way she wants them to be
extracts.summary: Beverly wants separated parent/teacher sections - doing upload/delete workaround

extracts.quote: people like the tables being created for them especially for rating scales. likes being able to just upload the data vs. plugging everything in, having the baseline info already there is helpful
extracts.summary: Beverly notes team likes auto-generated tables for rating scales - upload vs manual entry

extracts.quote: her campus has a specific parent and teacher input form, where the check boxes seemingly are confusing our system
extracts.summary: Beverly's campus-specific forms with checkboxes confuse the system

extracts.quote: Tess suggested we create forms for parents and teachers that would be consistent across the district and work better with Marker
extracts.summary: Tess suggests standardized district forms designed to work with Marker

extracts.quote: when the platform is wrong, populates information incorrectly it can be harder to use and take more time to correct things that it should get right but when it does work it is saving time - overall thinks its unreliable
extracts.summary: Karen says fixing platform errors takes more time than manual - considers it unreliable
